## Understanding the Evaluation Metrics {#sec-exp-metrics}

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Plain-Language Summary
Before presenting the experiment results, this section explains what the
evaluation metrics actually measure and why we chose them. This is
particularly important for a 1,926-class problem where the choice of
averaging method significantly affects what the number means — and whether
it honestly reflects the system's capabilities.
:::

---

### Top-1 Accuracy

The simplest metric: of all test records, what fraction did the model
predict correctly?

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{Correct predictions}}{\text{Total predictions}} = \frac{811}{966} = 83.9\%$$

**What it tells you:** How often the system gets the right answer.

**What it conceals:** Whether errors are evenly distributed across all
1,926 codes, or concentrated in a small number of difficult ones.

In multi-class classification, accuracy is equivalent to the **micro F1
score** — it pools all predictions together and measures overall volume
of correct answers. A model that perfectly predicts the 100 most common
codes and fails on the remaining 1,826 could still achieve high accuracy
if the common codes dominate the test set.

In our case the test set is **uniform** (approximately 5 records per code),
so accuracy is a fair metric here. On real clinical data — where hypertension,
diabetes, and back pain dominate — it would be dangerously misleading.

---

### F1 Score: The Building Block

**F1 score** for a single class is the harmonic mean of precision and recall:

$$F1 = 2 \times \frac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

Where precision and recall are computed using a **One-vs-Rest** approach —
each class is treated as a binary classification problem in turn:

- **Precision** — of all the times the model predicted this code, how
  often was it correct? A high-precision model avoids false alarms.
- **Recall** — of all the records that truly had this code, how many
  did the model find? A high-recall model avoids missed detections.

For a concrete example using `I10` (Essential hypertension):

| | Predicted I10 | Predicted other |
|---|---|---|
| **True I10** | 5 TP | 1 FN |
| **True other** | 0 FP | 960 TN |

$$\text{Precision}_{I10} = \frac{5}{5+0} = 1.00 \qquad
  \text{Recall}_{I10} = \frac{5}{5+1} = 0.833 \qquad
  F1_{I10} = 0.909$$

This per-class F1 is computed separately for all 1,926 codes. The question
is then: how do we average 1,926 F1 scores into a single number?

---

### Three Averaging Strategies — and Why They Give Different Answers

There are three standard approaches, and the differences between them
matter enormously for a 1,926-class problem:

**Macro average** computes the simple unweighted mean across all classes —
every class counts equally regardless of how many examples it has:

$$\text{Macro F1} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} F1_i = \frac{F1_{I10} + F1_{M25.562} + \cdots + F1_{Z79.4}}{1926}$$

**Weighted average** weights each class by its number of examples (its
*support*). Classes with more training examples contribute more to the
final score:

$$\text{Weighted F1} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\text{support}_i}{\text{total}} \times F1_i$$

**Micro average** pools all True Positives, False Positives, and False
Negatives across all classes before computing F1. For single-label
classification this is mathematically identical to accuracy:

$$\text{Micro F1} = \text{Accuracy} = \frac{\sum TP_i}{\sum (TP_i + FP_i)} = 83.9\%$$

The table below summarises when each is appropriate:

| Averaging | Treats classes | Best when |
|---|---|---|
| **Macro** | Equally | All classes matter equally regardless of frequency |
| **Weighted** | Proportionally to frequency | Common classes matter more |
| **Micro** | By prediction volume | Only overall throughput matters |

---

### Why This Project Uses Macro F1

**Macro F1 is the right choice here for two reasons that reinforce each other.**

First, **all 1,926 ICD-10 codes are clinically equally important.** A rare
code for a serious condition — say `A06.1` (chronic intestinal amoebiasis)
— is no less significant than a common code for a routine condition like
`M25.562` (pain in left knee). Getting the rare code wrong has the same
consequence as getting the common code wrong. Macro averaging encodes this
equal importance directly.

Second, **the training set is deliberately uniform.** MedSynth has
approximately 5 records per code by design. In this setting, weighted
averaging would converge toward macro averaging anyway — but using macro
explicitly makes the equal-weight intention transparent.

::: {.callout-important}
## Macro F1 vs Accuracy: Why They Tell Different Stories Here

For this project both metrics are consistent because the test set is uniform.
On real clinical data they would diverge significantly:

Consider a model that masters the 50 most common ICD-10 codes (covering
roughly 60% of real encounters) but fails on the remaining 1,876 codes.

- **Accuracy** (= Micro F1): ~60% — looks acceptable
- **Macro F1**: ~0.03 — catastrophically poor

Macro F1 exposes the failure that accuracy hides. This is why Macro F1
is the primary metric in this project and why it should be the primary
metric for any automated coding system evaluated for clinical deployment.
:::

**What Macro F1 = 0.762 means in practice:** Averaged equally across all
1,926 ICD-10 codes — from the highest-performing circulatory codes (~0.98)
to the most difficult administrative codes (~0.62) — the model achieves
an F1 of 0.762. This is a strong result for a 1,926-class problem with
~4 training examples per class. Random guessing on this task would produce
a Macro F1 close to zero.

---

### Expected Calibration Error (ECE)

ECE measures whether the model's confidence scores are trustworthy.
A perfectly calibrated model that says "70% confident" is correct
exactly 70% of the time.

ECE is computed by bucketing predictions into confidence bins and measuring
the gap between average confidence and average accuracy in each bin:

$$\text{ECE} = \sum_{b=1}^{B} \frac{|B_b|}{n} \left| \text{acc}(B_b) - \text{conf}(B_b) \right|$$

| ECE | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| < 0.05 | Excellent — confidence scores are reliable |
| 0.05 – 0.15 | Good — minor overconfidence |
| 0.15 – 0.30 | Moderate — needs treatment |
| > 0.30 | Poor — confidence scores not meaningful |

E-010 achieves **ECE = 0.034** after temperature scaling — excellent
calibration that makes the confidence threshold operationally meaningful.
Before calibration, the ECE was 0.640 — the model was severely overconfident
and the threshold could not be trusted.

---

### Coverage@τ and Accuracy on Covered

These two metrics jointly describe the **selective automation** capability:

- **Coverage@τ** — the fraction of test records where the model's top-1
  confidence exceeds the threshold τ. At τ = 0.7: 82.1% of records are
  auto-coded.

- **Accuracy on covered** — the precision on the auto-coded subset.
  At τ = 0.7: 95.2% of auto-coded predictions are correct.

Together they define the operating point: *how much can we automate, and
at what quality?* A lower threshold automates more but at lower precision;
a higher threshold automates less but at higher precision. The τ = 0.7
operating point was chosen as a practical balance point — see
@sec-results-automation for the full threshold sweep.
